findings.title: http→https upgrade redirect no longer sends Authorization header
findings.detail: RFC 7235 §2.2 only mandates stripping the Authorization header when the origin (scheme+host+port) changes AND the new origin is not considered 'more trusted'. Upgrading from http to https on the same host (a very common redirect pattern, e.g. HSTS pre-loading) is generally considered a safe upgrade, but this change will now strip the Authorization header in that case. The PR strips auth for *any* scheme change, including http→https on the same host and port. This is overly conservative and will silently break authentication for the common http-to-https redirect pattern. At minimum this deserves a comment and/or a more targeted fix (strip only on https→http downgrades, not on upgrades).

findings.title: scheme comparison is case-sensitive and may miss http→HTTP redirects
findings.detail: urlparse().scheme is already lowercased by Python's urllib.parse, so in practice this is safe. However, the comment in the code says "canonical root URL" but the comparison doesn't normalise the scheme. In practice Python's urlparse does lowercase the scheme, so this specific concern is low risk, but the port comparison is a more real edge case: when a URL omits a default port (e.g. https://host vs https://host:443), urlparse().port returns None for the first and 443 for the second. These two URLs are semantically identical but this comparison would incorrectly strip the Authorization header because None != 443. Similarly for http://host vs http://host:80. This is a behavioural regression for anyone who redirects between scheme://host and scheme://host:default_port.

findings.title: No test for port-change-only redirect
findings.detail: The PR adds a port comparison to the condition in `rebuild_auth`, but there is no corresponding test that verifies auth is stripped when a redirect changes only the port number (e.g. http://host:8080 → http://host:9090). This leaves a new code path untested.

findings.title: New test only covers https→http downgrade, not http→https upgrade
findings.detail: The test `test_auth_is_stripped_on_scheme_redirect` redirects from `httpbin_secure` (https) to `httpbin` (http), which is the security-relevant direction. There is no test for the reverse (http→https on the same host) to confirm whether auth is intentionally stripped or retained in that case. Since the implementation strips auth in both directions, at minimum a test (or xfail) for the http→https case should exist to document the design decision.

findings.title: No unit test for default-port vs explicit-port edge case
findings.detail: Given the port comparison uses `urlparse().port` (which returns `None` for omitted ports), a redirect from `https://host/path` to `https://host:443/path` would result in `None != 443` being `True`, causing the Authorization header to be stripped even though the effective origin is the same. There is no test covering this edge case.

This is overly conservative and will silently break authentication for the common http-to-https redirect pattern.
